summary: Patrick McKenzie walks through his first year of tax preparation as a solo software business owner, detailing which expenses he deducted and which he didn't for his Bingo Card Creator side business. He reports $2,480.05 in gross income on his Schedule C-EZ and methodically evaluates each potential deduction with the help of a CPA friend. The post reveals the mundane reality of running a tiny software business — penny-pinching over $4.50 tax savings on a WinZip license while honestly acknowledging that his internet connection serves his World of Warcraft habit more than his business. He promises to share the final Schedule C-EZ overview once complete.
key_points: A first-year micro-ISV generated $2,480.05 in gross revenue, making tax preparation a useful learning exercise in business expense tracking
Honest categorization of deductions matters more than aggressive claiming — he skips plausible-but-dishonest deductions like home internet
Keeping copious records and reviewing old blog posts can surface forgotten deductible expenses like the $99 Robosoft purchase
Even tiny tax savings ($4.50 on WinZip) add up when you're methodical about tracking every business expense
money_quotes: You can come to great tax savings if you keep copious records.
I have a level 60 gnome mage who would argue violently that the 50MB/s connection I pay for is not business related.
Good thing I don't have to declare DKP as income.
$99 expenditure = $15 tax savings. Not bad for 2 minutes of work.
I had totally forgotten about this expenditure until I was browsing my blog's 'stats' category looking for stuff I had missed.
key_insight: Running a micro-ISV means sweating the small stuff on taxes — keeping meticulous records and being honest about personal-versus-business use turns even trivial expenses into legitimate deductions.
